Update:

So, as I see the problem, pgi auto converts ``cairo.Context`` to ``cairocffi.Context`` whereas with normal cairocffi you have to manually convert as the matplotlib code does in the code above, so it tries to convert an already converted context, and hence causes memory problems and a segfault.

Does that sound plausible as an explanation?  Can I test that?  Can we change that auto-conversion, or does that undermine the basis of pgi by introducing non-cffi objects.  If so then I will change the matplotlib codebase, either with a simple ``if USES_PGI == False: convert``, or better if ``if not is_instance(ctx, cairocffi.context.Context): # convert to cffi``.
With the latest changes committed to master it will always use the same datastructure that was passed in. So you can pass in an OrderedDict and the result will ordered as expected.

@nicodv I added the test cases in test_kmodes and test_kprototypes to show that the end-to-end flow works and the results are expected when using the Ng dissimilarity measure. 

After testing, I also submitted a bug fix that occurred when invoking fit and then trying to predict new data. I overlooked that we need to keep track of initial encoded feature vector for the attribute frequency calculation in Ng's dissimilarity measure. I implemented the patch. However, this means that the encoded FV will not be freed by the GC. An alternative solution is to build a map of attribute frequencies at the beginning, so that we can allow the encoded FV to be freed, but this adds unnecessary memory overhead if not predicting new data (e.g., if you're just invoking fit or fit_predict). Therefore, I believe that maintaining a reference to the encoded FV is the best choice.

Hi @KristofMorva, nice to see you're interested.

First up, a big disclaimer, as I'm pretty sure I broke at least KModes and the original API of this package for my own intentions. The fork is not production-ready __at-all__, but last time I checked (which is a while ago), it worked (meaning K-Prototypes worked).
With that out of the way: you don't need to do magic stuff to install the package, just make sure you have a compiler toolchain installed. I'll give an example below:

1. Make sure you have a Python 3 environment with `Cython` and `numpy` installed. I would not recommend a global install so I'd use a virtual environment. If you really want a global install you can skip this step.
```bash
mkdir kmodes_test && cd kmodes_test
python3 -m venv env
source env/bin/activate
```

2. Clone my fork and enter the repository 
```bash
git clone git@github.com:rphes/kmodes && cd kmodes
```

3. Install the package
```bash
pip install -e .
```

The `-e` flag for pip installs in editable mode, so no files are moved to the `site-packages` directory, but only a link is placed there so python knows where to find the stuff.

You can now import the package using

```python
import cluster
```
(I think I renamed it)
Usage is as it was, but your data can now be either a tuple of `(num, cat)`, a dict of `{'num': num, 'cat': cat}` or a pandas dataframe with categorical datatypes in it.

Check it out and let me know if it worked!

I agree with Ilya, the internal ASN.1 decoder of ldap3 is "tailored" for LDAP messages, and only for decoding them. Also it doesn't perform any constraint checking. Also it assumes that received data is correct and that the BER coding is strictly followed by the server. In fact the whole decoder is only 212 lines long (including license, comments and blank lines). In ldap3 the ASN.1 encoding  is still done with pyasn1 for its excellent way of defining the "productions" described in the LDAP RFCs. 

Anyway to perform a comparison we need the LDAP  "production" (RFC4511) defined for the asn1crypto, and I have not found them anywhere. I don't know if there is a way to convert the pyasn1 code definitions to asn1crypto or if it uses a different approach.

Finally checked back and decided to have a go at tracking this down.  It turns out the bug is caused by incorrect introspection data returned by gnome-keyring.  dbus-python refuses to dispatch the call because the introspection data provided by gnome-keyring says the method does not exist.  The bug was introduced in gnome-keyring 3.4 and fixed in this commit:  http://git.gnome.org/browse/gnome-keyring/commit/?id=5dccbe88eb94eea9934e2b7c83e818bd21be4fd2

It looks like it should be fixed in gnome-keyring 3.5, but I have not verified this.

Original comment by: David Huebel

You're right. `pytest-runner` doesn't do anything during install. In fact, it doesn't do anything during any built-in setuptools or distutils command. Instead, it provides its own setup command 'ptr'.

pytest-runner cannot be in test_requirements because it must be present before test_requirements is honored. It must necessarily already be installed in order to supply the setuptools plugin to supply the 'ptr' command (which in turn honors test_requirements and runs the tests). It's a shame that it must also be present for installing the package even though it's not invoked.

Perhaps something else can be done. Maybe it would make sense to only add the setup requirement when 'ptr' appears in sys.argv, although that feels hacky. What is the motivation for changing it?

If one adds the argument `dayfirst=True` the problem is solved:

`'date': parse(date, dayfirst = True),`

By default, privacy mode is enabled. When privacy mode is enabled, a bot will not receive all messages that people send to the group. It will only receive selected messages, e.g. those starting with a slash, those that @mention the bot by username, etc.

But privacy mode can be disabled, so that the bot will begin receiving all messages like an ordinary user.

Interesting. One general suggestion is to use multiprocessing, not threading, to run multiple consumers. Each KafkaConsumer multiplexes IO over multiple broker connections, so you wont get much from multiple threads given the GIL restriction to a single CPU core. Multiprocessing (or any other strategy that gets you access to multiple cores) is a much better strategy for parallel processing in kafka-python.

With respect to your actual issue, it *feels* to me like you may have published a very large message that is larger than the maximum message size accepted by your consumers. What version of kafka-python are you using?
It looks like there may also be a bug in lz4tools that could cause a crash during lz4 decompression (assuming #1024 fix is applied to kafka-python): https://github.com/darkdragn/lz4tools/issues/8

I don't think this behaviour is going to be changed in any fundamental way. Later versions of WhiteNoise may use the more efficient filesystem APIs available in Python 3.5 which should make things faster, but it will be still scan all static files on start up. This behaviour is designed for production settings where trading slower startup time for faster response time makes perfect sense.

If your tests don't involve static files then you can either remove the WhiteNoise middleware or just make sure that `STATIC_ROOT` points to an empty directory (as in the comments above).

For tests which _do_ involve static files, your best option is to try to only create a single application instance and run all your static file tests against that so you only pay the start up cost once.

In any case, if you're having performance issues always profile before investing engineering time in optimisations (I recommend playing with [SnakeViz](https://jiffyclub.github.io/snakeviz/) if you haven't already). Discovering your bottlenecks by accident is never fun ;)

`game.home` & `game.away` reflects the actual home & away teams that appeared in that game.

`p.player.team` on the other hand uses the team found in the player's metadata (from `nflgame.players`)  That metadata is a snapshot of the current team rosters.  It does not have any historical knowledge of what team a player played for in the past.

Since a ton of players that appeared 7 years ago are no longer in the league, they do not have a `p.player.team`.  Others no longer play for the team they appeared on in 2010.

Using a much more recent example...look at the Vikings game from week 1, 2016.  At that time, Adrian Peterson played for Minnesota.  Today, he plays for New Orleans.
```python
import nflgame
#games = nflgame.games(year=2010,week=6)
games = nflgame.games(year=2016, week=1, home='MIN', away='MIN', kind='REG')
players = nflgame.combine_game_stats(games)
TeamsFromGame = [[game.home,game.away] for game in games]
TeamsFromPlayerStats = [p.player.team for p in players]

for home, away in TeamsFromGame:
	print home
	print away

print '-'*25
for p in players:
	print p.player, 'Now:', p.player.team, 'Then:', p.team
```

Okay, so here's a fun question.

We're used to thinking of us having one LensGalaxy and one SourceGalaxy. This is easy too set up as two instances of classes.

If we have two lens galaxies at the same redshift, its still simple, we just have two instances of LensGalaxy. When we compute their deflection angles, we'll just have a function which goes over every instance of LensGalaxy, and computes the deflection angles for every component of the mass profile.

However, what happens when our two lens galaxies are at different redshifts? Now, our deflection angle calculate must go to the LensGalaxy with the lowest redshift, compute its deflection angles and trace those image pixels to the redshift of the higher redshift LensGalaxy (this amounts to using the deflection angles we computed and multiplying them by a ratio of angular diameter distances). At these newly traced coordinates (which no longer form a uniform grid like the observed image), we then calculate the deflection angles for LensGalaxy2, which can then be used to trace to the source plane for the reconstruction. 

Therefore, our LensGalaxy deflection angle computation routine must check the redshifts of the LensGalaxys and adjust its calculation accordingly (it probably makes sense to use two different routines, as we may want to approximate all lenses to be at the same redshift). Obviously, this calculate can extend for 3, 4, 5 etc. LensGalaxys.

By the same token, we must also remember that if two sources at two different redshifts are both strongly lensed, we may infact have two SourceGalaxys (each with their own source-plane for reconstruction). import geocoder

Thanks for the link about the dbase version and the list of cpg codepage values, they should prove very useful @micahcochrain! 

But now I'm curious. When i was looking over the specs it actually seems like PyShp is built around v7 of dbase. Its handling of true false values uses space for missing as in v7, rather than "?" as in v3-5. But more importantly it reads numeric and float fields as padded strings as in v7, rather than as actual binary doubles and floats as in v3-5. And since pyshp has worked so nicely for all this time one could only assume they must have been v7 right? See the format specs for [v7](http://www.dbase.com/Knowledgebase/INT/db7_file_fmt.htm) VS for [v3-5](http://www.oocities.org/geoff_wass/dBASE/GaryWhite/dBASE/FAQ/qformt.htm). 

Maybe since the version was never specified in the esri shapefile spec it really could be any dbase version as @ArtiiP suggested? And maybe by chance the majority of shapefiles have simply been written with the newest dbase version? 

At any rate, encoding read and write support now works smoothly, along with docs and tests, so that the user is always working with unicode, on both py2 and 3. So the last piece of the puzzle that would really complete pyshp's unicode support is to check the language flag or the cpg file. We can also check for the dbase version flag (which I believe is in the header) just to make sure it's v4 or higher.

Hi @SioKCronin , great work!

I realized that using the `operator` module is practical. I admit I haven't used much from that standard lib before. The implementation of the interface (from the example in the docstring) is really nice, much better than how I would implement it.

As for your questions,

1. I think it's fine to set upfront the objective function and number of iterations whenever they're doing the search. In my opinion, this forces the user to "create separate environments" for what they are searching for. I'm more for making these "search environments" as atomic as possible. My wary is that if we put them on the "optimize" method, some inadvertent state changes may occur.

2. Creating a mock-up class for an optimizer during testing is a good idea 👍, but how do you plan to implement it? Can this be done by creating a setUp fixture instead? 😄 

Just a few comments in terms of style and structure:

1. __On structure.__ I think it would be better if we put all search utilities inside a `pyswarms.utils.search` module, this means we add another directory `/search` then just put all GridSearch, RandomSearch and other mixins inside that. The `pyswarms.utils.functions` is more for objective functions (single objective, multi-obj, etc). Perhaps my use of `functions` as a module name is quite confusing? 😕 

2. __A few notes on docstrings.__ For `grid_search.py`, we can move the whole docstring (the one with the example) __from__ the class `GridSearch` __into__ the higher level docstring before you imported the modules. 

3. __More on docstrings.__ It will also be better (and will render nicely in the documentation once built) if we add some docstrings in our methods. For example, in `__init__`, we can set an `Attributes` header to describe the required attributes when initializing the class. In this library, we are following the [numpydocs](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/HOWTO_DOCUMENT.rst.txt) style guide. But you can just check the docstrings in one of the modules (like `global_best.py` or `local_best.py`) as a reference. It may look like work but it's enjoyable!

4. __Just a matter of style.__ I think from Python 2.2, the old-style declaration of classes is not recommended anymore. So you can define your `GridSearch` as `class GridSearch(object)`. You can refer to these references (Warning: it's a rabbit hole! [(1)](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4015417/python-class-inherits-object)[(2)](https://docs.python.org/release/2.2.3/whatsnew/sect-rellinks.html))

5. __Private attributes and methods.__ For consistency with other classes, I think it's fine to just remove the underscore in our attributes in the `GridSearch` class. It's fine to use `self.optimizer` than `self._optimizer` and etc. 

Lastly, it may be helpful, when you go about writing `RandomSearch` to check if there are areas of code that are redundant. It may be more efficient to create a `BaseSearch` class where `GridSearch` and `RandomSearch` will inherit, only overriding the `optimize()` method where they both differ.

All in all, this is great work and I'm really thankful for your help!

If we store the gradient unnormalized, and rescale between [-128, 127], small values of the gradient (small as in the norm is small, say in the range [0, 3]), the directions will be heavily discretized, giving non smooth lighting effects. If we store instead the spherical angles for the direction, it wil always have the same resolution and requires just 2 components, and we can use the 3rd component for the gradient norm (which is what we need for the TF anyway). So I think going to spherical coordinates for the gradient may be better. Hope that explains it a bit more.

Are you trying to run two different django projects on a sigle database using two different database schemas? If so, it won't work unfortunately. django-pyodbc-azure doesn't (and I guess the other standard Django database backends also don't) support such use cases.

Django database backends dont't provide official functionality to use multiple database schemas even in a single project.
If you try to migrate two different projects into a single database, you will observe unexpected behaviors on them.

You might be able to change database schema for your project from the default to one another by changing default schema for a database user, but it doesn't mean you can host multiple Django projects on a single database.
I recommend that you create a dedicated database per your Django project.

It's a clever solution. I think you could spare some code by using settings. Assuming the list of language is that of your website, you could do:

```python
from django.conf import settings

class LangCountryFilter(SimpleListFilter):
    title = 'country' 
    parameter_name = 'country'

    def lookups(self, request, model_admin):
        return settings.LANGUAGES

    def queryset(self, request, queryset):
        language = self.value()
        if language:
            queryset = MyModel.objects.language(language)
        return queryset
```    

Note however that this filter will discard any previously applied filter when there is a language defined, because it recreates the queryset from scratch. I don't really have a solution for this atm.

I wrestled with this and read through all these replies for a long while and finally came up with a (probably not the best) solution. I needed threads because my web client makes a bunch of little AJAX calls in parallel and I am using Flask and didn't want to fiddle with figuring out the event soup.  So I'm using uwsgi with threading for Flask.

So I just wrote a little middleware wrapper, so I run my Flask app in threads and it sends an 'emit' HTTP call to the same eventlet wsgi server that's serving out socket.io.

So I just do a post to serialize the message to the right thread.  Flask is on port XXXX and this listens on portYYYY.

Would be awesome if I could just somehow do sio.emit() from any thread, though...

```
sio = socketio.Server(async_mode='eventlet')
class Middleware:
    def __init__(self, app):
        self.wrapped_app = app

    def __call__(self, environ, start_response):
        if environ['REQUEST_METHOD'] == 'POST' and environ['PATH_INFO'].startswith('/emit'):
            try:
                length = int(environ.get('CONTENT_LENGTH', '0'))
            except ValueError:
                length = 0
            if length != 0:
                body = environ['wsgi.input'].read(length)
                obj = json.loads(body.decode('utf-8'))
                if 'message' in obj and 'body' in obj:
                    sio.emit(obj['message'], obj['body'])
            status = '200 OK'
            response_headers = [('Content-type', 'text/plain')]
            start_response(status, response_headers)
            return ['OK']
        return self.wrapped_app(environ, start_response)

wsapp = socketio.Middleware(sio)
wsapp = Middleware(wsapp)
...
def ws_server():
    eventlet.wsgi.server(eventlet.listen(('', port)), wsapp)

ws_thread = threading.Thread(target=ws_server)
ws_thread.daemon = True
ws_thread.start()
```

Hi @marcoabreu - this is more of a Python issue on the engineering side than something that can be fixed from within the website or docs systems. I might even label it a bug.

The problem happens because of unordered dict used in NDArrayIter
```
batch = mx.io.NDArrayIter(data={'data1': np.ndarray((1, 3, 20, 20)),                                                                                                                                               
                                'data2': np.ndarray((1, 3, 20, 20)),                                                                                                                                               
                                'data3': np.ndarray((1, 3, 10, 10)),                                                                                                                                               
                                'data4': np.ndarray((1, 3, 10, 10))})  
```
The problem is solved if  we change the dict to list.
So if there are multiple inputs, it would better use list instead of dict in Iterator.
I close the issue since issue is solved.

I don't seem to be getting my point across. If a property is missing that is specified in a swagger JSON body parameter, bravado-core forces the value to None, [setting it on the unmarshalled object even if it wasn't passed in the request](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/master/bravado_core/unmarshal.py#L138-L142). 

My reading of the spec says If a property is not required it is *optional*. Placing missing properties into the unmarshalled object (even with value None) renders them indistinguishable from sent properties.

I've asked repeatedly, with this behavior how would I implement PATCH? If you think that through I hope you can see the problem: it is impossible to determine which properties were sent in the partial object since you cannot simply filter out properties with value None, as None may be a valid value sent from a client. 

I cannot find this behavior specified in the swagger spec and I believe bravado-core is overreaching with an assumption that a missing property should be added with value None. To be clear:

*I want any missing property from the request to be **not present** in the unmarshalled object.*

If not globally, there should be minimally a parameter or configuration option that turns off this behavior.

After thinking for a bit, there's no reason why that script has to be used via `git mergetool`. As I learned during iterations, we can generate all the necessary files from the conflicted state:

https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Tools-Advanced-Merging#_manual_remerge

```bash
git show :1:example.yaml # Contents of `$BASE`
git show :2:example.yaml # Contents of `$LOCAL`
git show :3:example.yaml # Contents of `$REMOTE`
```

With respect to keeping the tool agnostic (e.g. agnostic to `git`/`hg`/`svg`), I don't think it's plausible for `git` as we need to find its `mergetool` configuration.

Ok, I ran this through a javascript debugger and found the culprit. In updateTaskCategory, you're setting taskMap[data.name] = category. Unfortunately, data.name is the family name, so all tasks of the same family end up being labeled with the same category. So because a few of my tasks are upstream pending and upstream disabled, everything in the same family with lower priority categories gets filtered out. This happens even though they were never added to the higher priority categories. So stuff just gets randomly removed from the visualizer.

There's also another issue with how you're computing mostImportantCategory. In order for mostImportantCategory to work properly, you need to know whether a task belongs in a higher priority category, but you perform category updates in a different order than the priority. You should probably have a constant to ensure that the priority order and the update order are the same. You don't really need to do this though, as you'll only see conflicts between upstream and regular categories. So you can change the logic to be more explicit about that and only have to worry about updating upstream categories first. You probably also only need to apply these to PENDING, as all other categories will supersede any upstream status.

A quick and dirty function for those who need that URL builder.
```
def get_endpoint_for_s3_bucket(bucket_name, region='eu-west-1'):
	import botocore.loaders
	import botocore.regions
	loader = botocore.loaders.create_loader()
	data = loader.load_data("endpoints")
	resolver = botocore.regions.EndpointResolver(data)
	endpoint_data = resolver.construct_endpoint("s3", region)
	parts = endpoint_data['hostname'].split("-")
	parts.insert(1, "website")
	url_postfix = "-".join(parts)
	url = "{bucket_name}.{url_postfix}".format(bucket_name=bucket_name, url_postfix=url_postfix)
	return url
```

Can this code tell the difference between something that was installed outside of `.eggs/` (e.g. in the system python) and something that has been installed in `.eggs/`?

It seems to be that it is important to differentiate the following two cases when considering the `my-pkg >= 2` dependency:
* system python has `my-pkg == 1`, `.eggs` does not have `my-pkg` (yet). Result should be: `my-pkg >= 2` gets installed in `.eggs/`.
* system python does not have `my-pkg`, `.eggs` already has `my-pkg == 1` (due to a dependency installed just before, e.g. from `setup_requires`). Result should be: VersionConflict exception.

Case 1 is the case we're trying to solve. Case 2 is the case I'm afraid we might be breaking.

Shebang lines are supported by the kernel, not by the shell. And the kernel can be pretty simple.

I believe there is one Unix flavour that insists on hash, bang, no more than 32 arbitrary characters, newline. And then uses the characters between ```#!``` and the newline as a completely uninterpreted filename (so backslashes are treated as actual backslashes, spaces as actual spaces, etc). On that version, no form of excaping is needed, but equally no form works. Compare that to other versions which allow arguments and terminate the executable name with a space - some of those have escaping conventions. There is no valid way of satisfying both of those sets of rules simultaneously.

As I say, my knowledge is limited, so I wouldn't take the above as guaranteed - but I believe that's the gist of the problem (basically it's insoluble in a platform-independent way).

In Chinese at least (can't speak for Japanese/Korean as well), ordinal numbers are prefixed with 第。
So，
 `第一 first`
`第二 second`

These will never appear in a `yyyy年mm月dd日` context.  You will commonly see

`第一個月 the first month` (*not* January, just an arbitrary month)
`第一年 the first year` 
`第一天 the first day`

but they don't refer to absolute dates/times, only relative. Does that make sense?

@amitripshtos Are you using a topic with more than one partition? If so, Kafka itself will not provide strong guarantees about message ordering. Each partition is a total ordering of messages, but the random way in which any client will choose which partition to read from makes the topic as a whole only a partial ordering. If you really want to be sure that your messages are totally ordered, you should use a topic with a single partition. However, doing so means you'll lose a lot of the stability and replication benefits that Kafka provides.

@cdeil  Thanks for noticing the use of `pylab`.  I don't use it either -- I must have blindly copied it from another doc page (it was used in several pages, probably propagating from a single source).  In any case, `pylab` is now gone from all `photutils` docs.  Thanks.

Yes, the window keyword isn't easy to describe in a few words, so I added a link from `create_matching_kernel` back to the narrative docs.  Thanks.

Additional guidance about selecting a particular `window` function is more difficult.  It seems to be more of an art than science.  I think your approach of playing around with the window until the  resulting kernel convolved with the source "best" matches the target PSF is the correct approach.  In general, I would start with the `CosineBellWindow`.  That's what the IRAF psfmatch task used:
http://stsdas.stsci.edu/cgi-bin/gethelp.cgi?psfmatch

Here are some real examples that also used a cosine bell window function:
http://dirty.as.arizona.edu/~kgordon/mips/conv_psfs/conv_psfs.html

There is a detailed paper (http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2011PASP..123.1218A) about creating matching kernels, which I will add to the docs.  Equation 10 is the Fourier ratio method I implemented in `create_matching_kernel`.  Equation 11 describes a `SplitCosineBell` window function, which is the most general form for a window function.  All of the other windows I implemented (`Hanning`, `Tukey`, `CosineBell`, and `TopHat`) are special forms of the `SplitCosineBell`.

If you are concerned about performance and applying Healpix functions in ufunc style, you should consider using a pybind11 interface (https://github.com/pybind/pybind11). Compared to cython, this is much, much cleaner, and, as far as I can tell, way more efficient.

If you want to play around with a chealpix-like Python Healpix library, you can have a look at https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/ift/pyHealpix.

I have another problem with Notice.__init__, where it attempts to fetch the last exc_info() to get a traceback, regardless if it is currently in an exception handler or not.  The result is that if do something like:

try:
    raise Exception('blah')
except:
    pass

airbrake('Write unrelated error to airbrake')

Then my message gets overwritten with 'blah' and the stack trace points to the handled exception.

It would be more useful if __init__ only searched for exc_info if it was passed an exception object, or otherwise had a way of invoking the string-only code path.

This came to light because gunicorn frequently has a "Resource temporarily unavailable" exception in exc_info, and calling the airbrake library anywhere after that will report it instead of the string error provided.

The whole point is that ceph-deploy does what the admin tells it to do, 
and nothing else.  Of course it iterates over teh list of hosts or devices 
the admin tells it to install on.  It's not (and isn't meant to be) 
something more omnicient.

The main thing I can think of in ceph-deploy that is state-dependent is 
where it detects the distro and acts accordingly.  We'll have to add a bit 
more (at least temporarily) to deal with the ceph version so that we do 
sysvinit for hammer and systemd for >= infernalis.  That is the kind of 
state that we *shouldn't* persist, though, because it can change at 
any time for any number of reasons, and ceph-deploy should not make 
assumptions.

Hmm, not a fan of adding a method to do the serialization since that's a key part of the functionality of the formatter and allowing it to be overwritten is kind of a foot gun. For example this change would make fixing #10 a lot harder since users would need to know how to use a custom encoder to get the consistent output they expect.

I would suggest to implement a `PrettyAndSafeJSONEncoder` class that sets the `sort_keys=True` and `indent=2` variables in its `__init__` (sadly no class attributes allowed to override that in a subclass) and then add a `PrettyJsonLogFormatter` class to the `dockerflow.logging` module which uses that encoder. Of course that would still require the abstraction of the serialization, which we should do with a "private" method `_serialize` to indicate that it's not to be overridden in a subclass.

I know it's a lot more code than this patch, but it's worth the effort to makes sure the formatter isn't abused ;)

I must say that indeed contributing back to Invenio 1 was quite complicated and that could explain why a company basing its product back in the days was having difficult to contribute back (one was forced to hack business logic deep into the core of Invenio and this could have been problematic to share). A company based on Invenio 3 could have its core business in internal components that don't need to be contributed upstream, while it wouldn't cost anything to simply keep public forks of those Invenio component that are employed in once project, since these will very likely not contain any sensible private business logic or secret value of the company.

If I understand correctly, you're building the package yourself, which means you're running `make` command at some point. If you could provide the console output of that command, it may help understand the problem.

In terms of resolving it, you may try the following steps:
- Find where the `libomp.dylib` library is on your system (usually it can be found at `{LLVM_INSTALLATION_FOLDER}/lib/libomp.dylib`)
- Copy or sym-link that library into `/usr/local/opt/llvm/lib/libomp.dylib`

If for some reason your llvm installation folder does not contain `libomp.dylib`, then either you have a corrupt or very old llvm library. You can check the version with `{LLVM_INSTALLATION_FOLDER}/bin/llvm-config --version`.

I just pushed a new fix that I like better.  It moves the fix farther upstream to the PoissonDeviate class itself.  Now whenever it gets a mean > 2^30, it internally uses a `normal_distribution` for its implementation rather than the boost `poisson_distribution`.  

This way we don't have to worry about the same problem cropping up somewhere else with large means being given to PoissonDeviate.  Also, it afforded me the ability to write unit tests for this fix.  (I wasn't so keen on the unit test being that a photon shooting routine runs for 12 minutes rather than crashing.)

Simple way to fix it: use the cache buster.
When you include FontAwesome CSS like this:

`<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="/path/to/font-awesome.css" />`

Append `?v=1234` to the end of the value in `href` so it becomes like this:

`<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="/path/to/font-awesome.css?v=1234" />`

Done!
... but in my case, I appended it with a PHP `date()` value so that the "cache buster" value always changes:

`<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="/path/to/font-awesome.css?v=<?= date('YmdHis') ?>" />`

Hope it helps.

Somehow postgres received two shutdown requests. But only one of then actually was initiated by patroni, the last one (fast shutdown).
Replica stopped streaming right after the first shutdown request (smart) and didn't got latest changes. Actually it should be solved by pg_rewind, but somehow it was not called...

Now we need to figure out who and why sends smart shutdown request. I guess it was sent by `systemctl`

```
KillMode=

    Specifies how processes of this unit shall be killed. One of control-group, process, mixed, none.

    If set to control-group, all remaining processes in the control group of this unit will be killed on unit stop (for services: after the stop command is executed, as configured with ExecStop=). If set to process, only the main process itself is killed. If set to mixed, the SIGTERM signal (see below) is sent to the main process while the subsequent SIGKILL signal (see below) is sent to all remaining processes of the unit's control group. If set to none, no process is killed. In this case, only the stop command will be executed on unit stop, but no process be killed otherwise. Processes remaining alive after stop are left in their control group and the control group continues to exist after stop unless it is empty.

    Processes will first be terminated via SIGTERM (unless the signal to send is changed via KillSignal=). Optionally, this is immediately followed by a SIGHUP (if enabled with SendSIGHUP=). If then, after a delay (configured via the TimeoutStopSec= option), processes still remain, the termination request is repeated with the SIGKILL signal (unless this is disabled via the SendSIGKILL= option). See kill(2) for more information.

    Defaults to control-group.
```

At least in our example we didn't changed KillMode, so probably it defaults to `control-group`.
If you are using not the latest version of patroni (which doesn't include https://github.com/zalando/patroni/commit/7b524bc5571f2c49d7375d2cbd4eb3c7d303dca8), then it could explain such behaviour.

In any case please try to update your service file and set `KillMode=process`, hopefully it should help.

Non, on fait les màj quand on a des modifs à faire. Si on passe par ce système, c’est justement pour ne pas subir les mises à jour systématiques et parfois incohérentes, du type de celle qui est à l’origine de cette issue.

Encore une fois, replaçons la solution dans le contexte du problème. La solution normalement employée par la communauté dans le genre de cas qui nous occupe est la publication de paquets stables sur un registre public, numérotés avec semver.

Le meta-repo est une implémentation alternative de l’explication de dépendances à plusieurs modules en même temps. Il s’agit donc toujours d’un moyen pour un produit client de pointer vers plusieurs références en même temps. De la même manière qu’on mettrait à jour volontairement un numéro de version dans un fichier de définition de dépendances plutôt que de le subir, on ne met à jour le meta-repo que lorsqu’un produit a un intérêt à incorporer les modifications.

Notre objectif ici n’est pas de maintenir des pointeurs vers l’ensemble des états stables d’OpenFisca (ne serait-ce que parce qu’il n’y a pas de changelog et que nous n’avons pas les ressources à investir dans de la rétro-ingénierie), mais de maintenir des pointeurs vers des états cohérents utilisés par nos produits à des moments donnés.
Chaque produit utilisant cette implémentation comme moyen de maintenir des états cohérents doit gérer les commits à chaque mise à jour.

Si vous voulez maintenir cet historique, pas de souci pour vous laisser la main sur `master` et utiliser de notre côté des branches nommées par produit  :)
Definitely in favor of being `ZIP_SAFE` if possible.

@shinku721 看了一下wikipedia的描述，不知理解对不。hash由强度足够的哈希算法对user/password、时间戳(30秒内算同一个时间戳）等上传的参数算出。这个TOTP每30秒算一个timestamp的话，加上前后30秒，服务端算出三个hash，比对客户端上传的hash，时间超过30秒的就会一个都匹配不到。30秒内的才会验证通过。  
[这里能想到的问题就是hash是特定算法生成，在足够量的情况下，会不会统计出什么规律了，或者hash碰撞得到原始参数的问题，如果hash的参数再加上未加密的data一起算出应该会好些。

这样又回到OTA了，OTA的SHA1_HMAC  hash本来只对data的，如果再加上上述的时间戳一起算出这个hash，就不用添加字段了，而且hash也是藏在加密数据里的。对于OTA来说每个包都有hash，把第一个包的IV和hash省为一个IV（其实也是hash）也没多大必要。（才发现本讨论顶上第一条就是这个TOTP的描述－ －）

非OTA模式，那么IV由上述hash算法生成，服务器再校检一下，也不需要改字段，就能防止replay了。
另外我觉得，

对墙来说，OTA为了完整性的做法，如果说是为了抗干扰，那如果收到一串假的数据和一串真的数据拼在一起返回给应用，应用也没办法知道恰好丢弃掉假的那一部分而有可能是全给丢了，然后这条连接也一样坏掉，跟被墙直接RST没啥两样了。
如果是为了防止内容修改，那么至少你的密码已经泄漏了，才能被准确的插入修改内容，普通人也享受不到这待遇，而且这也超出了shadowsocks的所承诺的安全性保障，作为一款普通的翻墙不应该在其之上作太多事，匿名和加密应该交给别的应用挂在代理上用。

@Spareo one thing to keep in mind - if you keep changing the name of the stack, stacker will not remove the old stack automatically for you - it only keeps track of the stacks in it's config.  So if you start with `namespace-my-stack-sha1` and then run build again with it changed to `namespace-my-stack-sha2`, it'll create a whole new stack, but the `sha1` stack will still remain.

This is to avoid stacker thinking it owns all the stacks in an account - though there's a PR right now for deleting stacks that are in stackers `namespace` but no longer in the config.

So what do you actually need? Do you need the actions that take an agent from point A to point B using "MoveAhead" and so on? Or do you just want the cost of the shortest path? I think I misunderstood before that you just wanted the cost, but the answer is still the same. The networkx package can give you a shortest cost path on a weighted or unweighted graph including the cost of the path as well as the nodes and edges of the graph. We have some internal code for running a breadth first search to generate the navigation graph which can be used by networkx to generate shortest paths, but it assumes a static room IE no opened doors or moved around objects. It does still implicitly consider obstacles if they couldn't be navigated through when generating the graph. You can also look into dynamically computing paths on the Unity side via https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/Navigation.html but that is beyond what we offer currently.

@predatell then developers have a choice: they patch and fork this library themselves, running all tests on their own, and only once they are sure that the software works as intended do they then release a version supporting `lxml==4.3.0`.

Otherwise, they need to wait for upstream to patch and release a tested version of their code.

Because it is unknowable if `4.3.0` only fixes the CVE, and not a single side effect changes this project, without running the tests.

Indeed, even fixing the CVE might cause this library to stop working entirely, requiring a potential rewrite or new library.

A great example of that, which is more benign, is if `lxml==4.3.0` **changes license to GPL** and downstream developers may not want GPL in their project!  Even something as simple as a license change impacts the world, and we can't just use new versions of software freely.

> it will not break anything if the idea to handle fields with variable length as proper even if it is larger than expected is implemented.

The thing that it breaks is the strict enforcement of the protocol. :)  The question at hand is: Should we enable behaviors that are non-protocol-compliant? In general, web3.py aims to reject invalid inputs and outputs, which helps us identify bugs and encourage consensus behavior.

One possible approach would be to have an optional middleware that truncates the extraData field before it is formatted into its "pythonic" type. Users could add that middleware when connecting to the dev chain.

_FWIW, if we don't care how long the field is, then we can just use `HexBytes()` directly, instead of using `to_hexbytes()`, which was written for a stricter limitation on the number of bytes allowed. Although we might want a cleaner type-checking error in `HexBytes()`, which seems better to have anyway._

> The get_reduced_index() method in conda/resolve.py is much more fine-grained than the more course get_reduced_index() function you've added this too. I think it's better to implement strict channel priority in the more fine-grained version.

Looking at the `get_reduce_index()` method in conda/resolve.py it is unclear to me how to effectively remove packages which appear in a higher priority channel inside the method.  Once an instance of the Resolve class is created, self.index contains all the packages which are to be considered.  Pruning this index outside of the class insures that the lower priority packages are never considered and the resolution would be faster.

This is part of conda 4.4+. The config parameter affecting this is “aggressive_update_packages” (I think).  The value defaults to openssl, certifi, and ca-certificates.

Documents in elasticsearch are not visible for search until a `refresh` operation happens, which defaults to once every second. Call `connection.indices.refresh()`to trigger the operation manually.

See https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/guide/current/near-real-time.html for more information.

As a quick hack I implemented this TCPConnector wrapper - is this a viable work around until a real fix can be implemented? or is calling the close method within the connect method likely to lead to worse issues:

```
class TCPConnectorWithTimeout(aiohttp.TCPConnector):
    def __init__(self, *args, max_connection_lifetime=40, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.__transports = WeakKeyDictionary()
        self.__max_connection_lifetime = max_connection_lifetime

    async def connect(self, request):
        while True:
            con = await super().connect(request)

            current_time = datetime.utcnow().timestamp()
            creation_time = self.__transports.get(con.transport, None)

            if not creation_time:
                self.__transports[con.transport] = current_time
            elif creation_time < (current_time - max_connection_lifetime):
                con.close()
                continue

            return con
```

@takluyver your point is certainly valid, but examining my own (bad) behavior I often don't even look at the failure before relaunching it once. The only time I do that is if I see that the same test group failed on multiple versions of Python, which suggests a real bug.

I dusted off my (possibly wrong) dice-rolling probabilities from ages ago to check out how likely a failure needs to be for this to hide it: https://nbviewer.jupyter.org/gist/minrk/a87b89aa46a24ea77b0f

If there's a failure in a single test group 50% of the time, the fact that we are running each group twice (py2, py3) means that these retries will reduce that from at-least-one failure in 75% of builds to at-least-one failure in 25% of builds, which is still noticeable. If it's affecting all the js test groups equally, it would still produce a single failure 65% of the time, as opposed to the current 99.6% of the time.

What this approach hides most is low-probability failures. For instance, a js failure affecting all js test groups 10% of the time will only show up 1% of the time after this change, rather than 60% of the time now.

In my mind it isn't a question of doing the same thing a different way, but
of refactoring the code to decouple these types of dependencies out of it.
In this case, the only part of the code base that show know anything about
the DOM elements of cells are the cell views. That part of the DOM tree
should be a private implementation detail of that view.

This is why starting with interfaces in the new code base is so important.
They specify a contract with other parts of the code base about what you
can and can't do with it.

Our existing code base is a big tangled mess of things like this - it is
going to be very difficult to decouple things in the existing notebook.

But as we design the new JS frontend, we are looking long and hard at this
type of coupling and trying to remove it in all cases. So far, I don't
think we have had to do this type of thing so that is a good sign.

How about [tox](https://tox.readthedocs.org/en/latest/)?

All you need is a `tox.ini` file that can be as simple as:

```
[tox]
envlist = py27, py33, py34, py35

[testenv]
commands = py.test
deps =
    pytest
    lib1
    lib2
    ...
```

Then just run `tox`. It creates virtualenvs and keeps them there to avoid reinstalling unchanged dependencies all the time. It's very configurable and has direct support from pytest to include pytest directives in `tox.ini`.

@feliksik it seems like there are a couple questions here, but I might be missing something, so let me know.

**Why wan't peru designed to create real clones of your dependencies?** The main problem peru sets out to solve is fetching relatively stable 3rd-party code. For that use case, there's certainly nothing *wrong* with creating real clones, but it turns out that there are more important features that are incompatible with doing that. See https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/issues/127 for the gory details.

**Why prefer a large repo with many projects, over smaller repos with tooling?** The tools that try to join small repos together run into problems when the repos change frequently, and these problems are very difficult to solve:

- Your commit history becomes difficult to read, because it's hard to tell what changed in a given dependency update.
- `git bisect` in the parent repo can't tell you which dependency commit caused a bug.
- Merge conflicts between two dependency updates can't be automatically resolved.

In contrast, here are the problems you run into in a big unified repo, which I think have good solutions:

- The repo gets too big, and `git` operations get slow.
  - On modern hardware, git supports repos with hundreds of thousands of files. If your repo is as large as Google's or Facebook's, you can use some of the Mercurial infrastructure that Facebook has [open sourced](https://code.facebook.com/posts/218678814984400/scaling-mercurial-at-facebook/) to scale up even more.
- It's hard to change dependencies without breaking callers.
  - This problem happens no matter what, and the difference in a unified repo is that you find about it more quickly. That's a good thing. You also gain the ability to make atomic changes in multiple callers and dependencies at the same time. If you find that breaks are happening too frequently, the best way to solve that is with automated testing that runs on every commit.
    - Though that's not *entirely* fair. One thing you can do with split repos is let every caller stay on a different dependency version and update at its own pace. If all of the callers are really completely unrelated to each other, that can work. But if you ever need to update everything at once (say you're changing your logging backend, and every project needs the updated logging library), this will bite you badly, because at update time you'll have to deal with all the bugs you put off.

Great idea, but unfortunately it can potentially break our product, so I’d like to describe our scenario:

My team works on an APM product which is basically a Profiler with IL Rewriting. We use the profiler API to inject our code with AssemblytRefs and TypeRefs to existing types into the customers application. Now these references can be trimmed, which leads to crashes.

Until this point our approach for .NET Core was that we only used references to Types that are part of Netstandard. We thought with that we are on the safe side, now it turns out this is not the case.

I looked at [this ](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/samples/linker-instructions-advanced.md) and I understand that we can exclude dlls from trimming, but we do our injection runtime, so our customers with this approach have to recompile their applications and we have to extra maintain the list of Types we use. This is extremely hard in our case -I’d say this is not feasible at all. (I can go into more details, but I guess you can imagine the situation…e.g.: what if we update our stuff, etc. ). 

Did you consider this scenario? The problem itself is similar to the problem with reflection I guess. Our goal would be that we don’t have to say that “this is an unsupported scenario”. Furthermore, crashes caused by this are I think very problematic and we should figure out a solution to at least prevent TypeLoad-crashes caused by this. 

Maybe one option that comes to my mind:

Would it be an option to implement a flag within the Linker that ensures that e.g. no Netstandard types will be removed? That way we can at least have a fix set of APIs that we can use and customers then compile the application with this single flag. Of course, we also should somehow handle scenarios where the application is not built with this flag.

https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/blob/229cd78df08a0778bf09511fd2014f2d198dfbce/telethon/telegram_bare_client.py#L635-L649

No sleeps. As little delay as possible. The `.receive` method is as straightforward as possible:
https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/blob/229cd78df08a0778bf09511fd2014f2d198dfbce/telethon/network/mtproto_sender.py#L132-L134

As soon as an update arrive it gets processed on the (updates) state:
https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/blob/229cd78df08a0778bf09511fd2014f2d198dfbce/telethon/network/mtproto_sender.py#L258-L259

Then one of many workers poll these queue:

> row-major means columns are stored contiguously

I'm sincerely not sure if that is how you would express this or not, but either way we can conclude that natural language is confusing and ambiguous, that we agree on what we _mean_, and we're aware of that now. I think that is what matters most. ;)

Anyway, back to the actual orientation discussion. My usual disclaimer: I do not do actual research with data. Since I do not know how the data is used in practice, I have no idea what would be the more logical orientation. 

However, I do have some ideas about _what matters when deciding_, and I hope those considerations are useful for the discussion. I won't touch the traditions/conventions argument, which should be considered but is obviously outside of my knowledge domain.

# 1: Should Genes or Cells be the major order?

First thing to note: whether genes or cells should be the major order is a separate question from whether we should make genes/cells row- or column- oriented. The former is about how the data is used. The second depends on the answer to the first question, and whether the platforms the data is used on are row- or column-major order.

The first question is obviously the more important one, and answering it requires more than just determining which data is accessed more often.

## Types of data access

The kind of access matters as much as how often it is accessed: it is faster to access 64 rows in sequence in one go, than accessing 64 random ones. We can distinguish _random access_ vs _whole blocks_. Major order matters for both, but it matters more for random access than block access.

From the comments made (and after a quick chat with @gioelelm) I gather that:

- genes are more likely to be individually accessed (more random access)
- cell access is more relevant for clustering algorithms, machine learning, etc, more likely to involve whole datasets in block access

(I could be way off here, please correct me if I am)

Assuming that this interpretation is correct, that would suggest that gene major order would benefit more.

## Data size

Another thing to consider is relative number of cells versus genes, and size of the data set.

I'm not a biologist, but as I understand, the number of genes is more or less fixed per organism (for example ~29k for the mouse data I've seen in Sten's group). The number of cells varies, and currently most sets I have seen in the group are less than 29k. However, this number will grow rapidly in the future, and into the millions.

If we have to choose, I would argue it is more important to optimise for the larger datasets (so total cells >>> total genes), because of the relative greater impact of performance improvements. Improving performance by 10% on a small data set might save minutes at best, whereas that 10% performance increase might save hours on larger data sets.

When total cells >>> total genes, cell major order would have a significantly more negative impact on gene access, than gene major order would have on cell access. This again speaks in favour of genes as major order. This applies mainly to datasets loaded in memory though, because of the next point.

Loom files are chunked, so major- and minor order should not matter regarding disk access; I just looked at the documentation, which had this to say: ["The chunks of the dataset are allocated at independent locations throughout the HDF5 file and a B-tree maps chunk N-dimensional addresses to file addresses."](https://support.hdfgroup.org/HDF5/doc/H5.user/Chunking.html#S5)

The HDF5 groups _does_ suggest [making chunk shapes asymmetrical when you know the data has a preferred order](https://support.hdfgroup.org/HDF5/doc/Advanced/Chunking/). In our case, we might want to make the chunks that contain more cells than genes. This would reduce disk access when acessing genes, and have relatively little cost when accessing cells (again assuming cells >>> genes).

# 2: After answering the previous question, what does that mean for orientation?

Let's say the answer to the previous question is that it makes more sense to make genes the major order.

Python is row-major order. Numpy defaults to row-major, but can support column-major. However, every other language used in the sciences is column major (Fortran, R, Matlab, and though nobody here seems to use this beautiful language: Julia).

I suspect that effectively, Fortran matters for libraries powering the rest (BLAS, LAPACK), and we shouldn't worry about it. Matlab I recall being used more physics and less in biology, so probably not that big of a deal either.

The big two contenders seem to be Python (with Numpy) and R.

We _could_ make genes column-aligned and force all of our Numpy code to use column-major order, which would make things consistent, but that would lead to terrible performance with other python packages that expects row-major data (presumably most).

For a while I considered a silly, somewhat blasphemous idea: let the Python and R loom libraries use whatever orientation would make genes the major order (_not_ the loom file itself, which should have a fixed orientation). This would make genes align with rows for Python, and with columns in R. It would have the convenient consequence that it aligns with the statistics traditions in R, and the genomics traditions in Python (only the Python stats and machine learning packages would be left out), meaning it fits expectations for most people (as long as they work stick to their own platform).

Of course, this would lead to confusion when you use both packages, and it would mean `row_attributes` and `col_attributes` switch from platform to platform, which would complicate naming conventions. Sounds problematic to me, but maybe it isn't as bad as I think.

I guess in the end the main consideration should be _how_ the Python and R platforms are used: is there more random access, or block access, and more gene or cell access?

Let's say that Python is typically used in a fashion that favours random access, and R in a way that favours whole-dataset block access. In that case, the benefit of making the genes row-aligned should be bigger in general. A similar argument holds if Python does more gene access an R does more cell access. 

But I have no idea about what this is like in practice, so I'll leave it at that.

Hope this was somewhat useful,
/Job

This could be documented somewhere, but at the moment, the `max`, `min`, and `stride` arguments to a BVS only affect results when using VSA. I haven't found a non-ugly way to have them automatically apply when using the symbolic domain.

Some ideas, for the record:

1. Use an ITE: `claripy.BVS('test', 8, max=42)` would become `If(test > 8, 8, test)`, with similar things for min and stride. This seems super ugly.
2. Have solvers automatically add these sorts of "implicit" constraints when you attempt to evaluate an expression (temporarily, via `extra_constraints`) or when you add an expression as part of a constraint (permanently). This seems a bit too magical, but is probably the better solution.

@tcr3dr @mrpollo The regular updates of point release notes are useful - they show exactly what has changed and help people understand whether updating makes sense. 

That said, I'm not sure they are the right thing for the release notes page. IMO the page is an opportunity for us to show off all the great new things we've been doing and that this release delivers. The point releases are too small to do this.

I *think* a rolled up release notes that captures all the changes in the current minor release up to the current pip release would be good (and this would roll with each pip release). So in this case a release note made up of everything from notes in 1.4.0 - 1.4.x.

Of course depends on how much effort. This wouldn't be my highest priority right now.

P.S. Arguably we also need a high level hand crafted note on what the API delivers in the current major version - API coverage etc. However that might be worth kicking off in 2.0 when we change the architecture.

This sounds like side-effects of USB buffer overflows. It happens whenever the host PC is not pulling USB buffers from the device fast enough. At the moment, librealsense just passes such partial frames to the application. 
This can be verified by printing out the value of `buf.bytesused` at [uvc-v4l2.cpp:344](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/src/uvc-v4l2.cpp#L344). 
During normal streaming this value should equal to `_buffers[buf.index].length`, but during overflows it would be lower. 
The overflows themselves are either caused by high CPU usage in parallel to streaming, or just a weak CPU (perhaps CPU is being thermal-throttled? In this case cooling can have big effect)
As a possible work-around I have previously recommended to increase the number of URBs in uvcvideo from 5 to about 8 (change `#define UVC_URBS 5` in `/drivers/media/usb/uvc/uvcvideo.h`, rebuild and replace the Kernel module). In theory this should increase the queue size between USB layer and UVC driver, reducing partial frames at expense of some extra latency. However, this might have other unexpected side-effects and would not be recommended in production environment. 

P.S. Just to make sure, you are using V4L back-end and not LIBUVC, correct?

Correct. v4l2-ctl actually reaches out to the camera and parses the UVC desciptors. librealsense consciously does something a little more 'dangerous' by hardcoding the subset of modes that are almost guaranteed to work. We've discussed it internally a few times and librealsense _may_ eventually support a get_stream_mode that actually asks the camera first.

Here's something I put together, which seem to work, but is certainly work in progress:

```python
import collections
from graphene_django import converter, utils

from graphene.types.utils import merge
from graphene.utils.is_base_type import is_base_type


def convert_fields(model, only_fields):
    model_fields = utils.get_model_fields(model=model)
    fields = collections.OrderedDict()

    for field in model_fields:
        name = field.name

        # Must be in only_fields
        if name not in only_fields:
            continue

        converted = converter.convert_django_field(field, None)
        if not converted:
            continue

        fields[name] = converted

    return fields


class DjangoModelInputMeta(type):

    @staticmethod
    def __new__(cls, name, bases, attrs):
        # We'll get called also for non-user classes like DjangoModelInput. Only
        # kick in when called for a sub-class.
        if not is_base_type(bases, DjangoModelInputMeta):
            return type.__new__(cls, name, bases, attrs)

        # Pop Meta info. Must be removed from class, otherwise graphene will
        # complain.
        meta = attrs.pop('Meta')
        fields = convert_fields(model=meta.model, only_fields=meta.only_fields)
        attrs = merge(attrs, fields)

        return type.__new__(cls, name, bases, attrs)


class DjangoModelInput(metaclass=DjangoModelInputMeta):
    """
    Derive a mutation's Input class from this and define a meta class with 
    `model` and `only_fields` members. This will populate the input class
    with the converted django members.
    """
    pass

```

I think the starting point would likely be the PR curves dashboard which has per-run sliders (in #471) which could perhaps be adapted into a global slider for the images dashboard, and the logic that hooks those sliders up to the individual charts would look similar.

I think perhaps more important than the actual implementation is defining the behavior of the user controls, things like:
- how does the global slider interact w/ individual per-chart sliders? (one simple option is that moving the global slider resets all per-chart sliders to that step value, but then moving a per-chart slider overrides it)
- how should it work when individual image charts go off-screen and then return, e.g. via pagination or when a run for that chart is selected, then deselected? should the slider value persist, or return to some other value?
- what happens if the global slider is set to a value at which a given chart doesn't have a sample?
- should there be a way to reset to the current default behavior of "show the last step for each chart?" note that if different charts have different last steps, then with a global slider there's no way to express that directly, so it would either need to be a separate toggle/button or a special "last step" position on the global slider, or something similar

It is as expected, an ABBA deadlock.  Luckily it looks relatively easy to fix by not taking the lock during the publish, but take it after when the MID is known and then either record the callback -> MID in the map as before, or call the callback inline if the paho client callback has already come through.

Some thoughts:

For Python 3.3+, this is something we can (and should) address.  It isn't specific to enums, though, but rather classes (this shows up for `enum_` because it inherits from `class_`).

The fix is for us to use `__qualname__` rather than `tp_name` when displaying the type in the docstring; that *should* get us the nested name, e.g. `Pet.Kind`.

That's an easy enough change, except it exposed something else that doesn't look right: our `__qualname__` values contain the module name, which they shouldn't.

E.g. a Python module `pymod.py`:
```Python
def foo():
    pass

class C():
    pass
```

Python:
```python
Python 3.6.3 (default, Oct  3 2017, 21:16:13) 
[GCC 7.2.0] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import pymod
>>> (pymod.foo.__name__, pymod.foo.__module__, pymod.foo.__qualname__)
('foo', 'pymod', 'foo')
>>> (pymod.C.__name__, pymod.C.__module__, pymod.C.__qualname__)
('C', 'pymod', 'C')
>>> (pymod.C.D.__name__, pymod.C.D.__module__, pymod.C.D.__qualname__)
('D', 'pymod', 'C.D')
```

while the equivalent using pybind11 is incorrectly tacking the module name into the `__qualname__`:
```
('D', 'mod', 'mod.C.D')
```

So this is going to take a bit more than just printing `__qualname__` instead of `tp_name`.

Fixes #3788 and #3709.

Other than exiting with 0 for success and non-zero for failure, Certbot actually doesn't use different exit codes to convey information to the user. Instead, Certbot allows you to pass in commands to be executed when renewal occurs. You can learn more about this with the output of `certbot --help renew` and can read more about why we made this decision [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/3262#issuecomment-231413027). In your case, you probably want to use `--renew-hook`.

Also, `certbot renew` should not exit with 1 when it does not need to update the certificates. If this is the code you're seeing, it suggests there is a problem. If you're getting this exit code without an error message written to stderr, please open a new ticket with the log of the issue.

I rather keep this out of the lib, building URIs is already done properly by the different frameworks we integrate with. In the case of Django, this library uses [request.build_absolute_uri(path)](https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-app-django/blob/03d51fc48a8a453e61a2652ee737fded241ddbcd/social_django/strategy.py#L140), which relies on [_get_raw_host()](https://github.com/django/django/blob/d1bab24e0144d14513a1411503c95ececb425188/django/http/request.py#L66), this method uses the following values (in the following order):

* `HTTP_X_FORWARDED_HOST`
  Header that can be set by `nginx` (quite common practice, and I fail to see why that it's restrictive to spawn several `my_portal` instances)
* `HTTP_HOST`
  Header sent by the client, in the case of `my_portal` the client is the `my_proxy`, this value can be set by `nginx` as well. 
* `SERVER_NAME`
  Environment value, you can set this in the `my_portal` environment values when running it.

Setting any of the values above should get you going, the good thing about the options above, is that it lets you reuse the `my_portal` instances and serve the same app under different hosts without any code change.

Another alternative is to override the built-in strategy and re-define the `build_absolute_uri()` method to use you custom value, then set the `SOCIAL_AUTH_STRATEGY` setting to point to your custom version.

Though perhaps slightly surprising, this is consistent with the rest of our treatment of `undefined` and I'm not eager to add special cases to our JSON serialization for this unless we have to.  For now, I think we should leave this as is, and see if it becomes a common pain point that really does need further special casing.

A few responses here:

## Updating
Instructions for updating are basically right. The steps should be 1/ shut down Ray on all nodes, 2/ write update script, 3/ run update script on head node, 4/ run update script in parallel on other nodes, 4/ start up Ray on all nodes. I agree that we should add these instructions to https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/doc/using-ray-on-a-large-cluster.md

# No AMI
This should work. Some suggestions on bug fixes, then I'll comment on whether it is a good idea. You can use the `-o` and `-e` options on `parallel-ssh` to redirect the standard output and standard error from each host to a file. This should help in debugging. Also, as written I think the script will just keep running even if individual commands result in errors (newline is like `;`, not like `&&`). It really would be best to have a script that won't have any errors along the way, though. That way we can just confirm that no error output was generated after running `parallel-ssh` and have confidence that the installation was successful.

I still tend to prefer steering users toward creating AMIs, but it is worth considering this. For one, the user doesn't have to get a smooth-running setup script. If there is a need for libraries that require license approval, large files, etc., it may be easier to just do it once, by hand, and then clone the result of this work. The larger worry that I have is that whenever there are external dependencies, e.g., downloading Anaconda or other dependencies, then speed and success become variable factors. Unless one has a good way to verify the success of the installation on each machine then this is a risky way to go. Note that these risks usually scale with the number of machines, so for small clusters the AMI may have less value, but as you get to larger installations it becomes increasingly useful to bring up all of the machines in a well-defined state.

Here's my guess at what happened. Someone installed jupyterlab using sudo, which created that `lab/extension` directory with root permissions. However, root doesn't search /usr/local/bin for executables. So `sudo` won't find jupyter, and normal users can't write to that directory to install extensions.

If that's the issue, you could set up root so that /usr/local/bin is in the path temporarily (security implications there), or you could fix the permissions on that /usr/local/share directory to let users write to it.

>All of Trio's time-keeping functionality is (currently) about monotonic / relative time.

Yeah, totally! This is how I originally implemented it as well.

>Do you think it's really the ability to control the speed of the clock that you want, or mainly just absolute clock support?

If I had to pick, mainly absolute clock support. Controlling the speed of the clock would be a nice side-effect of this, as it's easier, as a human, to think of time passing "in seconds". I'm not sure how you could increase the speed of the clock - but I have not poured over the source code enough yet to determine how the clock advances. I've just been playing around with a custom implementation that would allow me to do what I'm looking for.

> Do you have an example in mind where running it normally is useful?

A large part of my platform will involve scheduled callbacks, sometimes days/weeks in the future. As absolute time will be a large component of this, I have already modified your `MockClock` example and relegated all the annoying timezone, DST, etc interactions to the library [Pendulum](https://pendulum.eustace.io/docs/#introduction). The timezone bit is important as I would like to expose a scheduled time of `at_sunset` and `at_sunrise` and this naturally will change day-to-day across various timezones (I believe I can do this with the help of `astral`).

I don't believe I'm asking for anything from the project quite yet as I believe my use case can be handled by a custom implementation of the Clock!

> Since MockClock is intended to be predictable for testing, and since the epoch for monotonic time is totally arbitrary, it always starts at time 0.0.

The main benefit of setting it as `1.0` is that the clock would align itself with the start time of the call to `run`. That's all I was looking for at first. :smile:

@rbucker881 I wouldn't take offense to the initial 'read the Guide' comment - that's a trope of zeromq support, particularly on the IRC channel.  It's ~always the first response to questions, and meant to be quite friendly.

As for your test case, the second endpoint is most certainly valid, as described in [this section](http://zguide.zeromq.org/page:all#Plugging-Sockets-Into-the-Topology) of the Guide, specifically:

* [ØMQ connections] exist when a client does zmq_connect(3) to an endpoint, whether or not a server has already done zmq_bind(3) to that endpoint.

The messages are being queued in memory, waiting for the second server to bind.  This is why the client does not exit at the end of your script - it sits running, waiting for the second server to bind and receive its messages.  If you start a server on the second endpoint at any later time, it will receive its messages and the client will exit cleanly.

Another example: if you set HWM=1 on the client, then that's the maximum size of the in-memory queue, so your missing endpoint will only be assigned its first message, and your running one will get messages 1,3-9.  The default HWM is infinite, so messages keep being assigned to the second endpoint, trusting that it will come up eventually.
